On the grounds, there is a statue of Mori Tomonobu, a feudal retainer of the Fukuoka Domain, and one of the twenty-four Kuroda Horsemen. Below the statue, a folk song based on Mori called ‘[[Kuroda Bushi]]’ is engraved. There is also a sculpture of Nagamasa’s favored horned helmet...{{Cite web |url=https://www.terumojinja.com/ |title=光雲神社公式ホームページ |accessdate=2022-04-06 |publisher=光雲神社 |archive-date=2022-04-04 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220404154132/https://www.terumojinja.com/ |url-status=live }} The ceiling of the front shine is decorated with a painting by Shin Kihara called 謡鶴 (''utaidzuru'', ‘Singing Cranes’), which makes the call of a crane when an offering is given{{Cite web|url=https://dayori.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/161105/|title=中央区ものしり手帳　第３話 光雲神社のさい銭箱|accessdate=2022-06-01|publisher=[[福岡市]]|archive-date=2020-12-05|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201205173357/https://dayori.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/161105/|url-status=live}} In Kakiha Shrine, an auxiliary shrine of Terumo Shrine, Kuroda Shigetaka, [[Kuroda Mototaka]], Kuroda Tadayuki, Kuroda Tsunayuki, Atsuhime (daughter of Kuroda Tsugutaka and fiancée of Daigo Fuyuyoshi), and other related figures are enshrined as [[kami]]. The plaque on the front of the shrine bearing its name was designed by Kuroda Nagahisa, the fifteenth head of the [[Kuroda clan]]. The road to this shrine, affectionately known as ‘Nishi Park’s Sakura Path’ meets with Japan National Route 556, which passes between 3-chōme and 2-chōme of the Arato district of Fukuoka City{{Cite web |url=https://www.nishinippon.co.jp/item/n/777786/ |title=西公園さくら参道の紹介記事 |accessdate=2022-04-06 |publisher=[[西日本新聞]] |archive-date=2021-12-05 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211205132813/https://www.nishinippon.co.jp/item/n/777786/ |url-status=live }} ==History== {{multiple image | align = right | width = 220 | image1 = Cherry blossoms and torii of Aratsu Shrine in Terumo Shrine.JPG | caption1 = Aratsu Shrine in Terumo Shrine | image2 = Cherry blossoms and torii of Kakiwa Shrine in Terumo Shrine.JPG | caption2 = Kakiwa Shrine in Terumo Shrine }} In 1766, the sixth daimyō of the Fukuoka domain, Kuroda Tsugutaka, built a [[hokora]] in the outer citadel of Fukuoka Castle to worship Nagamasa. Soon afterwards, Yoshitaka was enshrined there as well. In 1871, the Kuroda family moved to [[Tokyo]] after the [[Abolition of the han system|abolition of the feudal domain system]] and the implementation of the [[Prefectures of Japan|prefecture system]]. Interested parties petitioned the eleventh daimyō, Kuroda Nagahiro, to move the shrine from inside the castle to downtown Fukuoka. In 1875, it was classified as a prefectural shrine. The shrine was moved again to its current location in Nishi Park in 1909. This location once had Aratoyama Tōshōgū, a shrine built by Kuroda Tadayuki. According to records of the Fukuoka Domain, the Aratoyama Tōshōgū had the most magnificent shrine buildings, surpassing the various [[Tōshōgū|Tōshō-gūs]] (shrines venerating [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]) across Japan. In 1945, the [[Bombing of Fukuoka]] completely destroyed Terumo Shrine. Documents, swords, and other treasures connected to the Kuroda family, and the main shrine building were lost in the resulting fires. In 1966, the shrine was rebuilt with concrete. ==References== {{Reflist}} == External links == ・[https://www.terumojinja.com/ 光雲神社]（公式サイト） [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]]{{Beppyo Shrines}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Terumo Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11 6]] [[en:Terumo Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Terumo Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Terumo Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11 6]] [[en:Terumo Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Terumo Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11 6]] [[en:Terumo Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subCategory]] [[Category:11]][[Category:Terumo Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Terumo Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Terumo Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Terumo Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Terumo Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]][[en:Terumo Shrine]][[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[en:Terumo Shrine]][[en:Terumo Shrine]][[en:Terumo Shrine]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:C111]]\[[Category:C111]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[sn:Terumo Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Terumo Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Fukuoka City (translation of 光雲神社)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Terumo Shrine
光雲神社 | image = [[File:Fukuoka Terumo shrine 2010A.jpg|250px]] | location = 13-1 Nishi Park, Chūō-ku, Fukuoka City, Fukuoka Prefecture {{location map|Japan Fukuoka Prefecture | alt = Terumo Shrine (Fukuoka Prefecture) | coordinates = {{coord|33|35|50|N|130|22|35|E}} }} | coordinates = {{Coord|33|35|50|N|130|22|35|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} {{Nihongo|'''Terumo Shrine'''|光雲神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Fukuoka City]]. Latterly located in Nishi Park in the [[Chūō-ku, Fukuoka|Chūō-ku ward]] of the city, the shrine and all contents were destroyed in 1945; a reconstruction in modern materials was erected in 1966. ==Summary== Terumo Shrine was classified as a [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines#Prefectural shrines|prefectural shrine]] but is now classified as a Beppyo shrine by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It enshrines [[Kuroda Yoshitaka]], the founder of the historical [[Fukuoka Domain]], and his son, [[Kuroda Nagamasa]], the first [[daimyo|daimyō]] of the domain. The name was derived from the second characters of the posthumous Buddhist names of those enshrined; 光 (light) from Yoshitaka, and 雲 (cloud) from Nagamasa. On the grounds, there is a statue of Mori Tomonobu, a feudal retainer of the Fukuoka Domain, and one of the twenty-four Kuroda Horsemen. Below the statue, a folk song based on Mori called ‘[[Kuroda Bushi]]’ is engraved. The plaque on the front of the shrine bearing its name was designed by Kuroda Nagahisa, the fifteenth head of the [[Kuroda clan]]. The road to this shrine, affectionately known as ‘Nishi Park’s Sakura Path’ meets with Japan National Route 556, which passes between 3-chōme and 2-chōme of the Arato district of Fukuoka City{{Cite web |url=https://www.nishinippon.co.jp/item/n/777786/ |title=西公園さくら参道の紹介記事 |accessdate=2022-04-06 |publisher=[[西日本新聞]] |archive-date=2021-12-05 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211205132813/https://www.nishinippon.co.jp/item/n/777786/ |url-status=live }} ==History== {{multiple image | align = right | width = 220 | image1 = Cherry blossoms and torii of Aratsu Shrine in Terumo Shrine.JPG | caption1 = Aratsu Shrine in Terumo Shrine | image2 = Cherry blossoms and torii of Kakiwa Shrine in Terumo Shrine.JPG | caption2 = Kakiwa Shrine in Terumo Shrine }} In 1766, the sixth daimyō of the Fukuoka domain, Kuroda Tsugutaka, built a [[hokora]] in the outer citadel of Fukuoka Castle to worship Nagamasa. Soon afterwards, Yoshitaka was enshrined there as well. In 1871, the Kuroda family moved to [[Tokyo]] after the [[Abolition of the han system|abolition of the feudal domain system]] and the implementation of the [[Prefectures of Japan|prefecture system]]. Interested parties petitioned the eleventh daimyō, Kuroda Nagahiro, to move the shrine from inside the castle to downtown Fukuoka. In 1875, it was classified as a prefectural shrine. The shrine was moved again to its current location in Nishi Park in 1909. This location once had Aratoyama Tōshōgū, a shrine built by Kuroda Tadayuki. According to records of the Fukuoka Domain, the Aratoyama Tōshōgū had the most magnificent shrine buildings, surpassing the various [[Tōshōgū|Tōshō-gūs]] (shrines venerating [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]) across Japan. In 1945, the [[Bombing of Fukuoka]] completely destroyed Terumo Shrine. Documents, swords, and other treasures connected to the Kuroda family, and the main shrine building were lost in the resulting fires. In 1966, the shrine was rebuilt with concrete. ==References== {{Reflist}} == External links == ・[https://www.terumojinja.com/ 光雲神社]（公式サイト） {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] ]] ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11 6]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subCategory]] [[Category:Terumo Shrine]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:C111]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Terumo Shrine]] [[en:Terumo Shrine]]

On the grounds, there is a statue of Mori Tomonobu, a feudal retainer of the Fukuoka Domain, and one of the twenty-four Kuroda Horsemen. Below the statue, a folk song based on Mori called ‘[[Kuroda Bushi]]’ is engraved. There is also a sculpture of Nagamasa’s favored horned helmet...{{Cite web |url=https://www.terumojinja.com/ |title=光雲神社公式ホームページ |accessdate=2022-04-06 |publisher=光雲神社 |archive-date=2022-04-04 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220404154132/https://www.terumojinja.com/ |url-status=live }} The ceiling of the front shine is decorated with a painting by Shin Kihara called 謡鶴 (''utaidzuru'', ‘Singing Cranes’), which makes the call of a crane when an offering is given{{Cite web|url=https://dayori.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/161105/|title=中央区ものしり手帳　第３話 光雲神社のさい銭箱|accessdate=2022-06-01|publisher=[[福岡市]]|archive-date=2020-12-05|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201205173357/https://dayori.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/161105/|url-status=live}} In Kakiha Shrine, an auxiliary shrine of Terumo Shrine, Kuroda Shigetaka, [[Kuroda Mototaka]], Kuroda Tadayuki, Kuroda Tsunayuki, Atsuhime (daughter of Kuroda Tsugutaka and fiancée of Daigo Fuyuyoshi), and other related figures are enshrined as [[kami]]. The plaque on the front of the shrine bearing its name was designed by Kuroda Nagahisa, the fifteenth head of the [[Kuroda clan]]. The road to this shrine, affectionately known as ‘Nishi Park’s Sakura Path’ meets with Japan National Route 556, which passes between 3-chōme and 2-chōme of the Arato district of Fukuoka City{{Cite web |url=https://www.nishinippon.co.jp/item/n/777786/ |title=西公園さくら参道の紹介記事 |accessdate=2022-04-06 |publisher=[[西日本新聞]] |archive-date=2021-12-05 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211205132813/https://www.nishinippon.co.jp/item/n/777786/ |url-status=live }} ==History== {{multiple image | align = right | width = 220 | image1 = Cherry blossoms and torii of Aratsu Shrine in Terumo Shrine.JPG | caption1 = Aratsu Shrine in Terumo Shrine | image2 = Cherry blossoms and torii of Kakiwa Shrine in Terumo Shrine.JPG | caption2 = Kakiwa Shrine in Terumo Shrine }} In 1766, the sixth daimyō of the Fukuoka domain, Kuroda Tsugutaka, built a [[hokora]] in the outer citadel of Fukuoka Castle to worship Nagamasa. Soon afterwards, Yoshitaka was enshrined there as well. In 1871, the Kuroda family moved to [[Tokyo]] after the [[Abolition of the han system|abolition of the feudal domain system]] and the implementation of the [[Prefectures of Japan|prefecture system]]. Interested parties petitioned the eleventh daimyō, Kuroda Nagahiro, to move the shrine from inside the castle to downtown Fukuoka. In 1875, it was classified as a prefectural shrine. The shrine was moved again to its current location in Nishi Park in 1909. This location once had Aratoyama Tōshōgū, a shrine built by Kuroda Tadayuki. According to records of the Fukuoka Domain, the Aratoyama Tōshōgū had the most magnificent shrine buildings, surpassing the various [[Tōshōgū|Tōshō-gūs]] (shrines venerating [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]) across Japan. In 1945, the [[Bombing of Fukuoka]] completely destroyed Terumo Shrine. Documents, swords, and other treasures connected to the Kuroda family, and the main shrine building were lost in the resulting fires. In 1966, the shrine was rebuilt with concrete. ==References== {{Reflist}} == External links == ・[https://www.terumojinja.com/ 光雲神社]（公式サイト） {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] ]] ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11 6]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subCategory]] [[Category:Terumo Shrine]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:C111]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Terumo Shrine]] [[en:Terumo Shrine]]

In 1871, the Kuroda family moved to [[Tokyo]] after the [[Abolition of the han system|abolition of the feudal domain system]] and the implementation of the [[Prefectures of Japan|prefecture system]]. Interested parties petitioned the eleventh daimyō, Kuroda Nagahiro, to move the shrine from inside the castle to downtown Fukuoka. In 1875, it was classified as a prefectural shrine. The shrine was moved again to its current location in Nishi Park in 1909. This location once had Aratoyama Tōshōgū, a shrine built by Kuroda Tadayuki. According to records of the Fukuoka Domain, the Aratoyama Tōshōgū had the most magnificent shrine buildings, surpassing the various [[Tōshōgū|Tōshō-gūs]] (shrines venerating [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]) across Japan. In 1945, the [[Bombing of Fukuoka]] completely destroyed Terumo Shrine. Documents, swords, and other treasures connected to the Kuroda family, and the main shrine building were lost in the resulting fires. In 1966, the shrine was rebuilt with concrete. ==References== {{Reflist}} == External links == ・[https://www.terumojinja.com/ 光雲神社]（公式サイト） {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subCategory]] [[Category:Terumo Shrine]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:C111]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Terumo Shrine]] [[en:Terumo Shrine]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Fukuoka City (translation of 光雲神社)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Terumo Shrine
光雲神社 | image = [[File:Fukuoka Terumo shrine 2010A.jpg|250px]] | location = 13-1 Nishi Park, Chūō-ku, Fukuoka City, Fukuoka Prefecture {{location map|Japan Fukuoka Prefecture | alt = Terumo Shrine (Fukuoka Prefecture) | coordinates = {{coord|33|35|50|N|130|22|35|E}} }} | coordinates = {{Coord|33|35|50|N|130|22|35|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} {{Nihongo|'''Terumo Shrine'''|光雲神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Fukuoka City]]. Latterly located in Nishi Park in the [[Chūō-ku, Fukuoka|Chūō-ku ward]] of the city, the shrine and all contents were destroyed in 1945; a reconstruction in modern materials was erected in 1966. ==Summary== Terumo Shrine was classified as a [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines#Prefectural shrines|prefectural shrine]] but is now classified as a Beppyo shrine by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It enshrines [[Kuroda Yoshitaka]], the founder of the historical [[Fukuoka Domain]], and his son, [[Kuroda Nagamasa]], the first [[daimyo|daimyō]] of the domain. The name was derived from the second characters of the posthumous Buddhist names of those enshrined; 光 (light) from Yoshitaka, and 雲 (cloud) from Nagamasa. On the grounds, there is a statue of Mori Tomonobu, a feudal retainer of the Fukuoka Domain, and one of the twenty-four Kuroda Horsemen. Below the statue, a folk song based on Mori called ‘[[Kuroda Bushi]]’ is engraved. There is also a sculpture of Nagamasa’s favored horned helmet...{{Cite web |url=https://www.terumojinja.com/ |title=光雲神社公式ホームページ |accessdate=2022-04-06 |publisher=光雲神社 |archive-date=2022-04-04 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220404154132/https://www.terumojinja.com/ |url-status=live }} The ceiling of the front shine is decorated with a painting by Shin Kihara called 謡鶴 (''utaidzuru'', ‘Singing Cranes’), which makes the call of a crane when an offering is given{{Cite web|url=https://dayori.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/161105/|title=中央区ものしり手帳　第３話 光雲神社のさい銭箱|accessdate=2022-06-01|publisher=[[福岡市]]|archive-date=2020-12-05|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201205173357/https://dayori.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/161105/|url-status=live}} In Kakiha Shrine, an auxiliary shrine of Terumo Shrine, Kuroda Shigetaka, [[Kuroda Mototaka]], Kuroda Tadayuki, Kuroda Tsunayuki, Atsuhime (daughter of Kuroda Tsugutaka and fiancée of Daigo Fuyuyoshi), and other related figures are enshrined as [[kami]]. The plaque on the front of the shrine bearing its name was designed by Kuroda Nagahisa, the fifteenth head of the [[Kuroda clan]]. The road to this shrine, affectionately known as ‘Nishi Park’s Sakura Path’ meets with Japan National Route 556, which passes between 3-chōme and 2-chōme of the Arato district of Fukuoka City{{Cite web |url=https://www.nishinippon.co.jp/item/n/777786/ |title=西公園さくら参道の紹介記事 |accessdate=2022-04-06 |publisher=[[西日本新聞]] |archive-date=2021-12-05 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211205132813/https://www.nishinippon.co.jp/item/n/777786/ |url-status=live }} ==History== {{multiple image | align = right | width = 220 | image1 = Cherry blossoms and torii of Aratsu Shrine in Terumo Shrine.JPG | caption1 = Aratsu Shrine in Terumo Shrine | image2 = Cherry blossoms and torii of Kakiwa Shrine in Terumo Shrine.JPG | caption2 = Kakiwa Shrine in Terumo Shrine }} In 1766, the sixth daimyō of the Fukuoka domain, Kuroda Tsugutaka, built a [[hokora]] in the outer citadel of Fukuoka Castle to worship Nagamasa. Soon afterwards, Yoshitaka was enshrined there as well. In 1871, the Kuroda family moved to [[Tokyo]] after the [[Abolition of the han system|abolition of the feudal domain system]] and the implementation of the [[Prefectures of Japan|prefecture system]]. Interested parties petitioned the eleventh daimyō, Kuroda Nagahiro, to move the shrine from inside the castle to downtown Fukuoka. In 1875, it was classified as a prefectural shrine. The shrine was moved again to its current location in Nishi Park in 1909. This location once had Aratoyama Tōshōgū, a shrine built by Kuroda Tadayuki. According to records of the Fukuoka Domain, the Aratoyama Tōshōgū had the most magnificent shrine buildings, surpassing the various [[Tōshōgū|Tōshō-gūs]] (shrines venerating [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]) across Japan. In 1945, the [[Bombing of Fukuoka]] completely destroyed Terumo Shrine. Documents, swords, and other treasures connected to the Kuroda family, and the main shrine building were lost in the resulting fires. In 1966, the shrine was rebuilt with concrete. ==References== {{Reflist}} == External links == ・[https://www.terumojinja.com/ 光雲神社]（公式サイト） {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subCategory]] [[Category:Terumo Shrine]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:C111]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Terumo Shrine]] [[en:Terumo Shrine]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Fukuoka City (translation of 光雲神社)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Terumo Shrine
光雲神社 | image = [[File:Fukuoka Terumo shrine 2010A.jpg|250px]] | location = 13-1 Nishi Park, Chūō-ku, Fukuoka City, Fukuoka Prefecture {{location map|Japan Fukuoka Prefecture | alt = Terumo Shrine (Fukuoka Prefecture) | coordinates = {{coord|33|35|50|N|130|22|35|E}} }} | coordinates = {{Coord|33|35|50|N|130|22|35|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} {{Nihongo|'''Terumo Shrine'''|光雲神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Fukuoka City]]. Latterly located in Nishi Park in the [[Chūō-ku, Fukuoka|Chūō-ku ward]] of the city, the shrine and all contents were destroyed in 1945; a reconstruction in modern materials was erected in 1966. ==Summary== Terumo Shrine was classified as a [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines#Prefectural shrines|prefectural shrine]] but is now classified as a Beppyo shrine by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It enshrines [[Kuroda Yoshitaka]], the founder of the historical [[Fukuoka Domain]], and his son, [[Kuroda Nagamasa]], the first [[daimyo|daimyō]] of the domain. The name was derived from the second characters of the posthumous Buddhist names of those enshrined; 光 (light) from Yoshitaka, and 雲 (cloud) from Nagamasa. On the grounds, there is a statue of Mori Tomonobu, a feudal retainer of the Fukuoka Domain, and one of the twenty-four Kuroda Horsemen. Below the statue, a folk song based on Mori called ‘[[Kuroda Bushi]]’ is engraved. There is also a sculpture of Nagamasa’s favored horned helmet...{{Cite web |url=https://www.terumojinja.com/ |title=光雲神社公式ホームページ |accessdate=2022-04-06 |publisher=光雲神社 |archive-date=2022-04-04 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220404154132/https://www.terumojinja.com/ |url-status=live }} The ceiling of the front shine is decorated with a painting by Shin Kihara called 謡鶴 (''utaidzuru'', ‘Singing Cranes’), which makes the call of a crane when an offering is given{{Cite web|url=https://dayori.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/161105/|title=中央区ものしり手帳　第３話 光雲神社のさい銭箱|accessdate=2022-06-01|publisher=[[福岡市]]|archive-date=2020-12-05|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201205173357/https://dayori.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/161105/|url-status=live}} In Kakiha Shrine, an auxiliary shrine of Terumo Shrine, Kuroda Shigetaka, [[Kuroda Mototaka]], Kuroda Tadayuki, Kuroda Tsunayuki, Atsuhime (daughter of Kuroda Tsugutaka and fiancée of Daigo Fuyuyoshi), and other related figures are enshrined as [[kami]]. The plaque on the front of the shrine bearing its name was designed by Kuroda Nagahisa, the fifteenth head of the [[Kuroda clan]]. The road to this shrine, affectionately known as ‘Nishi Park’s Sakura Path’ meets with Japan National Route 556, which passes between 3-chōme and 2-chōme of the Arato district of Fukuoka City{{Cite web |url=https://www.nishinippon.co.jp/item/n/777786/ |title=西公園さくら参道の紹介記事 |accessdate=2022-04-06 |publisher=[[西日本新聞]] |archive-date=2021-12-05 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211205132813/https://www.nishinippon.co.jp/item/n/777786/ |url-status=live }} ==History== {{multiple image | align = right | width = 220 | image1 = Cherry blossoms and torii of Aratsu Shrine in Terumo Shrine.JPG | caption1 = Aratsu Shrine in Terumo Shrine | image2 = Cherry blossoms and torii of Kakiwa Shrine in Terumo Shrine.JPG | caption2 = Kakiwa Shrine in Terumo Shrine }} In 1766, the sixth daimyō of the Fukuoka domain, Kuroda Tsugutaka, built a [[hokora]] in the outer citadel of Fukuoka Castle to worship Nagamasa. Soon afterwards, Yoshitaka was enshrined there as well. In 1871, the Kuroda family moved to [[Tokyo]] after the [[Abolition of the han system|abolition of the feudal domain system]] and the implementation of the [[Prefectures of Japan|prefecture system]]. Interested parties petitioned the eleventh daimyō, Kuroda Nagahiro, to move the shrine from inside the castle to downtown Fukuoka. In 1875, it was classified as a prefectural shrine. The shrine was moved again to its current location in Nishi Park in 1909. This location once had Aratoyama Tōshōgū, a shrine built by Kuroda Tadayuki. According to records of the Fukuoka Domain, the Aratoyama Tōshōgū had the most magnificent shrine buildings, surpassing the various [[Tōshōgū|Tōshō-gūs]] (shrines venerating [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]) across Japan. In 1945, the [[Bombing of Fukuoka]] completely destroyed Terumo Shrine. Documents, swords, and other treasures connected to the Kuroda family, and the main shrine building were lost in the resulting fires. In 1966, the shrine was rebuilt with concrete. ==References== {{Reflist}} == External links == ・[https://www.terumojinja.com/ 光雲神社]（公式サイト） {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subCategory]] [[Category:Terumo Shrine]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Terumo Shrine]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Terumo Shrine]] [[en:Terumo Shrine]]

On the grounds, there is a statue of Mori Tomonobu, a feudal retainer of the Fukuoka Domain, and one of the twenty-four Kuroda Horsemen. Below the statue, a folk song based on Mori called ‘[[Kuroda Bushi]]’ is engraved. There is also a sculpture of Nagamasa’s favored horned helmet...{{Cite web |url=https://www.terumojinja.com/ |title=光雲神社公式ホームページ |accessdate=2022-04-06 |publisher=光雲神社 |archive-date=2022-04-04 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220404154132/https://www.terumojinja.com/ |url-status=live }} The ceiling of the front shine is decorated with a painting by Shin Kihara called 謡鶴 (''utaidzuru'', ‘Singing Cranes’), which makes the call of a crane when an offering is given{{Cite web|url=https://dayori.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/161105/|title=中央区ものしり手帳　第３話 光雲神社のさい銭箱|accessdate=2022-06-01|publisher=[[福岡市]]|archive-date=2020-12-05|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201205173357/https://dayori.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/161105/|url-status=live}} In Kakiha Shrine, an auxiliary shrine of Terumo Shrine, Kuroda Shigetaka, [[Kuroda Mototaka]], Kuroda Tadayuki, Kuroda Tsunayuki, Atsuhime (daughter of Kuroda Tsugutaka and fiancée of Daigo Fuyuyoshi), and other related figures are enshrined as [[kami]]. The plaque on the front of the shrine bearing its name was designed by Kuroda Nagahisa, the fifteenth head of the [[Kuroda clan]]. The road to this shrine, affectionately known as ‘Nishi Park’s Sakura Path’ meets with Japan National Route 556, which passes between 3-chōme and 2-chōme of the Arato district of Fukuoka City{{Cite web |url=https://www.nishinippon.co.jp/item/n/777786/ |title=西公園さくら参道の紹介記事 |accessdate=2022-04-06 |publisher=[[西日本新聞]] |archive-date=2021-12-05 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211205132813/https://www.nishinippon.co.jp/item/n/777786/ |url-status=live }} ==History== {{multiple image | align = right | width = 220 | image1 = Cherry blossoms and torii of Aratsu Shrine in Terumo Shrine.JPG | caption1 = Aratsu Shrine in Terumo Shrine | image2 = Cherry blossoms and torii of Kakiwa Shrine in Terumo Shrine.JPG | caption2 = Kakiwa Shrine in Terumo Shrine }} In 1766, the sixth daimyō of the Fukuoka domain, Kuroda Tsugutaka, built a [[hokora]] in the outer citadel of Fukuoka Castle to worship Nagamasa. Soon afterwards, Yoshitaka was enshrined there as well. In 1871, the Kuroda family moved to [[Tokyo]] after the [[Abolition of the han system|abolition of the feudal domain system]] and the implementation of the [[Prefectures of Japan|prefecture system]]. Interested parties petitioned the eleventh daimyō, Kuroda Nagahiro, to move the shrine from inside the castle to downtown Fukuoka. In 1875, it was classified as a prefectural shrine. The shrine was moved again to its current location in Nishi Park in 1909. This location once had Aratoyama Tōshōgū, a shrine built by Kuroda Tadayuki. According to records of the Fukuoka Domain, the Aratoyama Tōshōgū had the most magnificent shrine buildings, surpassing the various [[Tōshōgū|Tōshō-gūs]] (shrines venerating [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]) across Japan. In 1945, the [[Bombing of Fukuoka]] completely destroyed Terumo Shrine. Documents, swords, and other treasures connected to the Kuroda family, and the main shrine building were lost in the resulting fires. In 1966, the shrine was rebuilt with concrete. ==References== {{Reflist}} == External links == ・[https://www.terumojinja.com/ 光雲神社]（公式サイト） {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Terumo Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subCategory]] [[Category:Terumo Shrine]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Terumo Shrine]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Terumo Shrine]] [[en:Terumo Shrine]] [[Category:New]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Fukuoka City (translation of 光雲神社)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Terumo Shrine
光雲神社 | image = [[File:Fukuoka Terumo shrine 2010A.jpg|250px]] | location = 13-1 Nishi Park, Chūō-ku, Fukuoka City, Fukuoka Prefecture {{location map|Japan Fukuoka Prefecture | alt = Terumo Shrine (Fukuoka Prefecture) | coordinates = {{coord|33|35|50|N|130|22|35|E}} }} | coordinates = {{Coord|33|35|50|N|130|22|35|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} {{Nihongo|'''Terumo Shrine'''|光雲神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Fukuoka City]]. Latterly located in Nishi Park in the [[Chūō-ku, Fukuoka|Chūō-ku ward]] of the city, the shrine and all contents were destroyed in 1945; a reconstruction in modern materials was erected in 1966. ==Summary== Terumo Shrine was classified as a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines#Prefectural shrines|prefectural shrine]] but is now classified as a Beppyo shrine by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It enshrines [[Kuroda Yoshitaka]], the founder of the historical [[Fukuoka Domain]], and his son, [[Kuroda Nagamasa]], the first [[daimyo|daimyō]] of the domain. The name was derived from the second characters of the posthumous Buddhist names of those enshrined; 光 (light) from Yoshitaka, and 雲 (cloud) from Nagamasa. On the grounds, there is a statue of Mori Tomonobu, a feudal retainer of the Fukuoka Domain, and one of the twenty-four Kuroda Horsemen. Below the statue, a folk song based on Mori called ‘[[Kuroda Bushi]]’ is engraved. There is also a sculpture of Nagamasa’s favored horned helmet...{{Cite web |url=https://www.terumojinja.com/ |title=光雲神社公式ホームページ |accessdate=2022-04-06 |publisher=光雲神社 |archive-date=2022-04-04 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220404154132/https://www.terumojinja.com/ |url-status=live }} The ceiling of the front shine is decorated with a painting by Shin Kihara called 謡鶴 (''utaidzuru'', ‘Singing Cranes’), which makes the call of a crane when an offering is given{{Cite web|url=https://dayori.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/161105/|title=中央区ものしり手帳　第３話 光雲神社のさい銭箱|accessdate=2022-06-01|publisher=[[福岡市]]|archive-date=2020-12-05|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201205173357/https://dayori.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/161105/|url-status=live}} In Kakiha Shrine, an auxiliary shrine of Terumo Shrine, Kuroda Shigetaka, [[Kuroda Mototaka]], Kuroda Tadayuki, Kuroda Tsunayuki, Atsuhime (daughter of Kuroda Tsugutaka and fiancée of Daigo Fuyuyoshi), and other related figures are enshrined as [[kami]]. The plaque on the front of the shrine bearing its name was designed by Kuroda Nagahisa, the fifteenth head of the [[Kuroda clan]]. The road to this shrine, affectionately known as ‘Nishi Park’s Sakura Path’ meets with Japan National Route 556, which passes between 3-chōme and 2-chōme of the Arato district of Fukuoka City{{Cite web |url=https://www.nishinippon.co.jp/item/n/777786/ |title=西公園さくら参道の紹介記事 |accessdate=2022-04-06 |publisher=[[西日本新聞]] |archive-date=2021-12-05 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211205132813/https://www.nishinippon.co.jp/item/n/777786/ |url-status=live }} ==History== {{multiple image | align = right | width = 220 | image1 = Cherry blossoms and torii of Aratsu Shrine in Terumo Shrine.JPG | caption1 = Aratsu Shrine in Terumo Shrine | image2 = Cherry blossoms and torii of Kakiwa Shrine in Terumo Shrine.JPG | caption2 = Kakiwa Shrine in Terumo Shrine }} In 1766, the sixth daimyō of the Fukuoka domain, Kuroda Tsugutaka, built a [[hokora]] in the outer citadel of Fukuoka Castle to worship Nagamasa. Soon afterwards, Yoshitaka was enshrined there as well. In 1871, the Kuroda family moved to [[Tokyo]] after the [[Abolition of the han system|abolition of the feudal domain system]] and the implementation of the [[Prefectures of Japan|prefecture system]]. Interested parties petitioned the eleventh daimyō, Kuroda Nagahiro, to move the shrine from inside the castle to downtown Fukuoka. In 1875, it was classified as a prefectural shrine. The shrine was moved again to its current location in Nishi Park in 1909. This location once had Aratoyama Tōshōgū, a shrine built by Kuroda Tadayuki. According to records of the Fukuoka Domain, the Aratoyama Tōshōgū had the most magnificent shrine buildings, surpassing the various [[Tōshōgū|Tōshō-gūs]] (shrines venerating [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]) across Japan. In 1945, the [[Bombing of Fukuoka]] completely destroyed Terumo Shrine. Documents, swords, and other treasures connected to the Kuroda family, and the main shrine building were lost in the resulting fires. In 1966, the shrine was rebuilt with concrete. ==References== {{Reflist}} == External links == ・[https://www.terumojinja.com/ 光雲神社]（公式サイト） {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subCategory]] [[Category:Terumo Shrine]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Terumo Shrine]] [[en:Terumo Shrine]]

On the grounds, there is a statue of Mori Tomonobu, a feudal retainer of the Fukuoka Domain, and one of the twenty-four Kuroda Horsemen. There is also a sculpture of Nagamasa’s favored horned helmet...{{Cite web |url=https://www.terumojinja.com/ |title=光雲神社公式ホームページ |accessdate=2022-04-06 |publisher=光雲神社 |archive-date=2022-04-04 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220404154132/https://www.terumojinja.com/ |url-status=live }} The ceiling of the front shine is decorated with a painting by Shin Kihara called 謡鶴 (''utaidzuru'', ‘Singing Cranes’), which makes the call of a crane when an offering is given{{Cite web|url=https://dayori.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/161105/|title=中央区ものしり手帳　第３話 光雲神社のさい銭箱|accessdate=2022-06-01|publisher=[[福岡市]]|archive-date=2020-12-05|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201205173357/https://dayori.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/161105/|url-status=live}} In Kakiha Shrine, an auxiliary shrine of Terumo Shrine, Kuroda Shigetaka, [[:en:Kuroda Mototaka|Kuroda Mototaka]], Kuroda Tadayuki, Kuroda Tsunayuki, Atsuhime (daughter of Kuroda Tsugutaka and fiancée of Daigo Fuyuyoshi), and other related figures are enshrined as [[kami]]. The plaque on the front of the shrine bearing its name was designed by Kuroda Nagahisa, the fifteenth head of the [[:en:Kuroda clan|Kuroda clan]]. The road to this shrine, affectionately known as ‘Nishi Park’s Sakura Path’ meets with Japan National Route 556, which passes between 3-chōme and 2-chōme of the Arato district of Fukuoka City{{Cite web |url=https://www.nishinippon.co.jp/item/n/777786/ |title=西公園さくら参道の紹介記事 |accessdate=2022-04-06 |publisher=[[西日本新聞]] |archive-date=2021-12-05 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211205132813/https://www.nishinippon.co.jp/item/n/777786/ |url-status=live }} ==History== {{multiple image | align = right | width = 220 | image1 = Cherry blossoms and torii of Aratsu Shrine in Terumo Shrine.JPG | caption1 = Aratsu Shrine in Terumo Shrine | image2 = Cherry blossoms and torii of Kakiwa Shrine in Terumo Shrine.JPG | caption2 = Kakiwa Shrine in Terumo Shrine }} In 1766, the sixth daimyō of the Fukuoka domain, Kuroda Tsugutaka, built a [[:en:Hokora|Hokora]] in the outer citadel of Fukuoka Castle to worship Nagamasa. Soon afterwards, Yoshitaka was enshrined there as well. In 1871, the Kuroda family moved to [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] after the [[:en:Abolition of the han system|abolition of the feudal domain system]] and the implementation of the [[:en:Prefectures of Japan|prefecture system]]. Interested parties petitioned the eleventh daimyō, Kuroda Nagahiro, to move the shrine from inside the castle to downtown Fukuoka. In 1875, it was classified as a prefectural shrine. The shrine was moved again to its current location in Nishi Park in 1909. This location once had Aratoyama Tōshōgū, a shrine built by Kuroda Tadayuki. According to records of the Fukuoka Domain, the Aratoyama Tōshōgū had the most magnificent shrine buildings, surpassing the various [[:en:Tōshōgū|Tōshō-gūs]] (shrines venerating [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]]) across Japan. In 1945, the [[:en:Bombing of Fukuoka|Bombing of Fukuoka]] completely destroyed Terumo Shrine. Documents, swords, and other treasures connected to the Kuroda family, and the main shrine building were lost in the resulting fires. In 1966, the shrine was rebuilt with concrete. ==References== {{Reflist}} == External links == ・[https://www.terumojinja.com/ 光雲神社]（公式サイト） {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] }} [[en:Terumo Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Terumo-jinja}} [[ja:光雲神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11388585}}

Latterly located in Nishi Park in the [[:en:Chūō-ku, Fukuoka|Chūō-ku ward]] of the city, the shrine and all contents were destroyed in 1945; a reconstruction in modern materials was erected in 1966. ==Summary== Terumo Shrine was classified as a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines#Prefectural shrines|prefectural shrine]] but is now classified as a Beppyo shrine by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. The name was derived from the second characters of the posthumous Buddhist names of those enshrined; 光 (light) from Yoshitaka, and 雲 (cloud) from Nagamasa. On the grounds, there is a statue of Mori Tomonobu, a feudal retainer of the Fukuoka Domain, and one of the twenty-four Kuroda Horsemen. Below the statue, a folk song based on Mori called ‘[[:en:Kuroda Bushi|Kuroda Bushi]]’ is engraved. There is also a sculpture of Nagamasa’s favored horned helmet...{{Cite web |url=https://www.terumojinja.com/ |title=光雲神社公式ホームページ |accessdate=2022-04-06 |publisher=光雲神社 |archive-date=2022-04-04 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220404154132/https://www.terumojinja.com/ |url-status=live }} The ceiling of the front shine is decorated with a painting by Shin Kihara called 謡鶴 (''utaidzuru'', ‘Singing Cranes’), which makes the call of a crane when an offering is given{{Cite web|url=https://dayori.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/161105/|title=中央区ものしり手帳　第３話 光雲神社のさい銭箱|accessdate=2022-06-01|publisher=[[福岡市]]|archive-date=2020-12-05|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201205173357/https://dayori.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/161105/|url-status=live}} In Kakiha Shrine, an auxiliary shrine of Terumo Shrine, Kuroda Shigetaka, [[:en:Kuroda Mototaka|Kuroda Mototaka]], Kuroda Tadayuki, Kuroda Tsunayuki, Atsuhime (daughter of Kuroda Tsugutaka and fiancée of Daigo Fuyuyoshi), and other related figures are enshrined as [[kami]]. The plaque on the front of the shrine bearing its name was designed by Kuroda Nagahisa, the fifteenth head of the [[:en:Kuroda clan|Kuroda clan]]. The road to this shrine, affectionately known as ‘Nishi Park’s Sakura Path’ meets with Japan National Route 556, which passes between 3-chōme and 2-chōme of the Arato district of Fukuoka City{{Cite web |url=https://www.nishinippon.co.jp/item/n/777786/ |title=西公園さくら参道の紹介記事 |accessdate=2022-04-06 |publisher=[[西日本新聞]] |archive-date=2021-12-05 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211205132813/https://www.nishinippon.co.jp/item/n/777786/ |url-status=live }} ==History== {{multiple image | align = right | width = 220 | image1 = Cherry blossoms and torii of Aratsu Shrine in Terumo Shrine.JPG | caption1 = Aratsu Shrine in Terumo Shrine | image2 = Cherry blossoms and torii of Kakiwa Shrine in Terumo Shrine.JPG | caption2 = Kakiwa Shrine in Terumo Shrine }} In 1766, the sixth daimyō of the Fukuoka domain, Kuroda Tsugutaka, built a [[:en:Hokora|Hokora]] in the outer citadel of Fukuoka Castle to worship Nagamasa. Soon afterwards, Yoshitaka was enshrined there as well. In 1871, the Kuroda family moved to [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] after the [[:en:Abolition of the han system|abolition of the feudal domain system]] and the implementation of the [[:en:Prefectures of Japan|prefecture system]]. Interested parties petitioned the eleventh daimyō, Kuroda Nagahiro, to move the shrine from inside the castle to downtown Fukuoka. In 1875, it was classified as a prefectural shrine. The shrine was moved again to its current location in Nishi Park in 1909. This location once had Aratoyama Tōshōgū, a shrine built by Kuroda Tadayuki. According to records of the Fukuoka Domain, the Aratoyama Tōshōgū had the most magnificent shrine buildings, surpassing the various [[:en:Tōshōgū|Tōshō-gūs]] (shrines venerating [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]]) across Japan. In 1945, the [[:en:Bombing of Fukuoka|Bombing of Fukuoka]] completely destroyed Terumo Shrine. Documents, swords, and other treasures connected to the Kuroda family, and the main shrine building were lost in the resulting fires. In 1966, the shrine was rebuilt with concrete. ==References== {{Reflist}} == External links == ・[https://www.terumojinja.com/ 光雲神社]（公式サイト） {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] }} [[en:Terumo Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Terumo-jinja}} [[ja:光雲神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11388585}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

[[sn:commons:Category:Terumo-jinja]][[en:Terumo Shrine]][[ja:光雲神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11388585]] Wikidata name: Terumo Jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Fukuoka City (translation of 光雲神社)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Terumo Shrine
光雲神社 | image = File:Fukuoka Terumo shrine 2010A.jpg | location = 13-1 Nishi Park, Chūō-ku, Fukuoka City, Fukuoka Prefecture {{location map|Japan Fukuoka Prefecture | alt = Terumo Shrine (Fukuoka Prefecture) | coordinates = {{coord|33|35|50|N|130|22|35|E}} }} | coordinates = {{Coord|33.59748611111111|N|130.3765388888889|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = {{ill|Chūō-ku|WD=Q1156273}}| founded_by = {{ill|Kuroda Tsugutaka|WD=Q11678674}}| deity = {{ill|Kuroda Nagamasa|WD=Q950496}}; {{ill|Kuroda Yoshitaka|WD=Q907019}}}} {{Nihongo|'''Terumo Shrine'''|光雲神社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Fukuoka City|Fukuoka City]]. Latterly located in Nishi Park in the [[:en:Chūō-ku, Fukuoka|Chūō-ku ward]] of the city, the shrine and all contents were destroyed in 1945; a reconstruction in modern materials was erected in 1966. ==Summary== Terumo Shrine was classified as a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines#Prefectural shrines|prefectural shrine]] but is now classified as a Beppyo shrine by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It enshrines [[:en:Kuroda Yoshitaka|Kuroda Yoshitaka]], the founder of the historical [[:en:Fukuoka Domain|Fukuoka Domain]], and his son, [[:en:Kuroda Nagamasa|Kuroda Nagamasa]], the first [[daimyo|daimyō]] of the domain. The name was derived from the second characters of the posthumous Buddhist names of those enshrined; 光 (light) from Yoshitaka, and 雲 (cloud) from Nagamasa. On the grounds, there is a statue of Mori Tomonobu, a feudal retainer of the Fukuoka Domain, and one of the twenty-four Kuroda Horsemen. Below the statue, a folk song based on Mori called ‘[[:en:Kuroda Bushi|Kuroda Bushi]]’ is engraved. There is also a sculpture of Nagamasa’s favored horned helmet...{{Cite web |url=https://www.terumojinja.com/ |title=光雲神社公式ホームページ |accessdate=2022-04-06 |publisher=光雲神社 |archive-date=2022-04-04 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220404154132/https://www.terumojinja.com/ |url-status=live }} The ceiling of the front shine is decorated with a painting by Shin Kihara called 謡鶴 (''utaidzuru'', ‘Singing Cranes’), which makes the call of a crane when an offering is given{{Cite web|url=https://dayori.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/161105/|title=中央区ものしり手帳　第３話 光雲神社のさい銭箱|accessdate=2022-06-01|publisher=[[福岡市]]|archive-date=2020-12-05|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201205173357/https://dayori.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/161105/|url-status=live}} In Kakiha Shrine, an auxiliary shrine of Terumo Shrine, Kuroda Shigetaka, [[:en:Kuroda Mototaka|Kuroda Mototaka]], Kuroda Tadayuki, Kuroda Tsunayuki, Atsuhime (daughter of Kuroda Tsugutaka and fiancée of Daigo Fuyuyoshi), and other related figures are enshrined as [[kami]]. The plaque on the front of the shrine bearing its name was designed by Kuroda Nagahisa, the fifteenth head of the [[:en:Kuroda clan|Kuroda clan]]. The road to this shrine, affectionately known as ‘Nishi Park’s Sakura Path’ meets with Japan National Route 556, which passes between 3-chōme and 2-chōme of the Arato district of Fukuoka City{{Cite web |url=https://www.nishinippon.co.jp/item/n/777786/ |title=西公園さくら参道の紹介記事 |accessdate=2022-04-06 |publisher=[[西日本新聞]] |archive-date=2021-12-05 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211205132813/https://www.nishinippon.co.jp/item/n/777786/ |url-status=live }} ==History== {{multiple image | align = right | width = 220 | image1 = Cherry blossoms and torii of Aratsu Shrine in Terumo Shrine.JPG | caption1 = Aratsu Shrine in Terumo Shrine | image2 = Cherry blossoms and torii of Kakiwa Shrine in Terumo Shrine.JPG | caption2 = Kakiwa Shrine in Terumo Shrine }} In 1766, the sixth daimyō of the Fukuoka domain, Kuroda Tsugutaka, built a [[:en:Hokora|Hokora]] in the outer citadel of Fukuoka Castle to worship Nagamasa. Soon afterwards, Yoshitaka was enshrined there as well. In 1871, the Kuroda family moved to [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] after the [[:en:Abolition of the han system|abolition of the feudal domain system]] and the implementation of the [[:en:Prefectures of Japan|prefecture system]]. Interested parties petitioned the eleventh daimyō, Kuroda Nagahiro, to move the shrine from inside the castle to downtown Fukuoka. In 1875, it was classified as a prefectural shrine. The shrine was moved again to its current location in Nishi Park in 1909. This location once had Aratoyama Tōshōgū, a shrine built by Kuroda Tadayuki. According to records of the Fukuoka Domain, the Aratoyama Tōshōgū had the most magnificent shrine buildings, surpassing the various [[:en:Tōshōgū|Tōshō-gūs]] (shrines venerating [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]]) across Japan. In 1945, the [[:en:Bombing of Fukuoka|Bombing of Fukuoka]] completely destroyed Terumo Shrine. Documents, swords, and other treasures connected to the Kuroda family, and the main shrine building were lost in the resulting fires. In 1966, the shrine was rebuilt with concrete. ==References== {{Reflist}} == External links == ・[https://www.terumojinja.com/ 光雲神社]（公式サイト） {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] }} [[en:Terumo Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Terumo-jinja}} [[ja:光雲神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11388585}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11388585}} [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

Soon afterwards, Yoshitaka was enshrined there as well. There is also a sculpture of Nagamasa’s favored horned helmet...{{Cite web |url=https://www.terumojinja.com/ |title=光雲神社公式ホームページ |accessdate=2022-04-06 |publisher=光雲神社 |archive-date=2022-04-04 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220404154132/https://www.terumojinja.com/ |url-status=live }} The ceiling of the front shine is decorated with a painting by Shin Kihara called 謡鶴 (''utaidzuru'', ‘Singing Cranes’), which makes the call of a crane when an offering is given{{Cite web|url=https://dayori.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/161105/|title=中央区ものしり手帳　第３話 光雲神社のさい銭箱|accessdate=2022-06-01|publisher=[[福岡市]]|archive-date=2020-12-05|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201205173357/https://dayori.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/161105/|url-status=live}} In Kakiha Shrine, an auxiliary shrine of Terumo Shrine, Kuroda Shigetaka, [[:en:Kuroda Mototaka|Kuroda Mototaka]], Kuroda Tadayuki, Kuroda Tsunayuki, Atsuhime (daughter of Kuroda Tsugutaka and fiancée of Daigo Fuyuyoshi), and other related figures are enshrined as [[kami]]. The plaque on the front of the shrine bearing its name was designed by Kuroda Nagahisa, the fifteenth head of the [[:en:Kuroda clan|Kuroda clan]]. The road to this shrine, affectionately known as ‘Nishi Park’s Sakura Path’ meets with Japan National Route 556, which passes between 3-chōme and 2-chōme of the Arato district of Fukuoka City{{Cite web |url=https://www.nishinippon.co.jp/item/n/777786/ |title=西公園さくら参道の紹介記事 |accessdate=2022-04-06 |publisher=[[西日本新聞]] |archive-date=2021-12-05 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211205132813/https://www.nishinippon.co.jp/item/n/777786/ |url-status=live }} ==History== {{multiple image | align = right | width = 220 | image1 = Cherry blossoms and torii of Aratsu Shrine in Terumo Shrine.JPG | caption1 = Aratsu Shrine in Terumo Shrine | image2 = Cherry blossoms and torii of Kakiwa Shrine in Terumo Shrine.JPG | caption2 = Kakiwa Shrine in Terumo Shrine }} In 1766, the sixth daimyō of the Fukuoka domain, Kuroda Tsugutaka, built a [[:en:Hokora|Hokora]] in the outer citadel of Fukuoka Castle to worship Nagamasa. Soon afterwards, Yoshitaka was enshrined there as well. In 1871, the Kuroda family moved to [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] after the [[:en:Abolition of the han system|abolition of the feudal domain system]] and the implementation of the [[:en:Prefectures of Japan|prefecture system]]. Interested parties petitioned the eleventh daimyō, Kuroda Nagahiro, to move the shrine from inside the castle to downtown Fukuoka. In 1875, it was classified as a prefectural shrine. The shrine was moved again to its current location in Nishi Park in 1909. This location once had Aratoyama Tōshōgū, a shrine built by Kuroda Tadayuki. According to records of the Fukuoka Domain, the Aratoyama Tōshōgū had the most magnificent shrine buildings, surpassing the various [[:en:Tōshōgū|Tōshō-gūs]] (shrines venerating [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]]) across Japan. In 1945, the [[:en:Bombing of Fukuoka|Bombing of Fukuoka]] completely destroyed Terumo Shrine. Documents, swords, and other treasures connected to the Kuroda family, and the main shrine building were lost in the resulting fires. In 1966, the shrine was rebuilt with concrete. ==References== {{Reflist}} == External links == ・[https://www.terumojinja.com/ 光雲神社]（公式サイト） {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] }} [[en:Terumo Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Terumo-jinja}} [[ja:光雲神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11388585}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11388585}} [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]]

[[sn:commons:Category:Terumo-jinja]][[en:Terumo Shrine]][[ja:光雲神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11388585]] Wikidata name: Terumo Jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Fukuoka City (translation of 光雲神社)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Terumo Shrine
光雲神社 | image = File:Fukuoka Terumo shrine 2010A.jpg | location = 13-1 Nishi Park, Chūō-ku, Fukuoka City, Fukuoka Prefecture {{location map|Japan Fukuoka Prefecture | alt = Terumo Shrine (Fukuoka Prefecture) | coordinates = {{coord|33|35|50|N|130|22|35|E}} }} | coordinates = {{Coord|33.59748611111111|N|130.3765388888889|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = {{ill|Chūō-ku|WD=Q1156273}}| founded_by = {{ill|Kuroda Tsugutaka|WD=Q11678674}}| deity = {{ill|Kuroda Nagamasa|WD=Q950496}}; {{ill|Kuroda Yoshitaka|WD=Q907019}}}} {{Nihongo|'''Terumo Shrine'''|光雲神社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Fukuoka City|Fukuoka City]]. Latterly located in Nishi Park in the [[:en:Chūō-ku, Fukuoka|Chūō-ku ward]] of the city, the shrine and all contents were destroyed in 1945; a reconstruction in modern materials was erected in 1966. ==Summary== Terumo Shrine was classified as a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines#Prefectural shrines|prefectural shrine]] but is now classified as a Beppyo shrine by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It enshrines [[:en:Kuroda Yoshitaka|Kuroda Yoshitaka]], the founder of the historical [[:en:Fukuoka Domain|Fukuoka Domain]], and his son, [[:en:Kuroda Nagamasa|Kuroda Nagamasa]], the first [[daimyo|daimyō]] of the domain. The name was derived from the second characters of the posthumous Buddhist names of those enshrined; 光 (light) from Yoshitaka, and 雲 (cloud) from Nagamasa. On the grounds, there is a statue of Mori Tomonobu, a feudal retainer of the Fukuoka Domain, and one of the twenty-four Kuroda Horsemen. Below the statue, a folk song based on Mori called ‘[[:en:Kuroda Bushi|Kuroda Bushi]]’ is engraved. There is also a sculpture of Nagamasa’s favored horned helmet...{{Cite web |url=https://www.terumojinja.com/ |title=光雲神社公式ホームページ |accessdate=2022-04-06 |publisher=光雲神社 |archive-date=2022-04-04 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220404154132/https://www.terumojinja.com/ |url-status=live }} The ceiling of the front shine is decorated with a painting by Shin Kihara called 謡鶴 (''utaidzuru'', ‘Singing Cranes’), which makes the call of a crane when an offering is given{{Cite web|url=https://dayori.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/161105/|title=中央区ものしり手帳　第３話 光雲神社のさい銭箱|accessdate=2022-06-01|publisher=[[福岡市]]|archive-date=2020-12-05|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201205173357/https://dayori.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/161105/|url-status=live}} In Kakiha Shrine, an auxiliary shrine of Terumo Shrine, Kuroda Shigetaka, [[:en:Kuroda Mototaka|Kuroda Mototaka]], Kuroda Tadayuki, Kuroda Tsunayuki, Atsuhime (daughter of Kuroda Tsugutaka and fiancée of Daigo Fuyuyoshi), and other related figures are enshrined as [[kami]]. The plaque on the front of the shrine bearing its name was designed by Kuroda Nagahisa, the fifteenth head of the [[:en:Kuroda clan|Kuroda clan]]. The road to this shrine, affectionately known as ‘Nishi Park’s Sakura Path’ meets with Japan National Route 556, which passes between 3-chōme and 2-chōme of the Arato district of Fukuoka City{{Cite web |url=https://www.nishinippon.co.jp/item/n/777786/ |title=西公園さくら参道の紹介記事 |accessdate=2022-04-06 |publisher=[[西日本新聞]] |archive-date=2021-12-05 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211205132813/https://www.nishinippon.co.jp/item/n/777786/ |url-status=live }} ==History== {{multiple image | align = right | width = 220 | image1 = Cherry blossoms and torii of Aratsu Shrine in Terumo Shrine.JPG | caption1 = Aratsu Shrine in Terumo Shrine | image2 = Cherry blossoms and torii of Kakiwa Shrine in Terumo Shrine.JPG | caption2 = Kakiwa Shrine in Terumo Shrine }} In 1766, the sixth daimyō of the Fukuoka domain, Kuroda Tsugutaka, built a [[:en:Hokora|Hokora]] in the outer citadel of Fukuoka Castle to worship Nagamasa. Soon afterwards, Yoshitaka was enshrined there as well. In 1871, the Kuroda family moved to [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] after the [[:en:Abolition of the han system|abolition of the feudal domain system]] and the implementation of the [[:en:Prefectures of Japan|prefecture system]]. Interested parties petitioned the eleventh daimyō, Kuroda Nagahiro, to move the shrine from inside the castle to downtown Fukuoka. In 1875, it was classified as a prefectural shrine. The shrine was moved again to its current location in Nishi Park in 1909. This location once had Aratoyama Tōshōgū, a shrine built by Kuroda Tadayuki. According to records of the Fukuoka Domain, the Aratoyama Tōshōgū had the most magnificent shrine buildings, surpassing the various [[:en:Tōshōgū|Tōshō-gūs]] (shrines venerating [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]]) across Japan. In 1945, the [[:en:Bombing of Fukuoka|Bombing of Fukuoka]] completely destroyed Terumo Shrine. Documents, swords, and other treasures connected to the Kuroda family, and the main shrine building were lost in the resulting fires. In 1966, the shrine was rebuilt with concrete. ==References== {{Reflist}} == External links == ・[https://www.terumojinja.com/ 光雲神社]（公式サイト） {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] }} [[en:Terumo Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Terumo-jinja}} [[ja:光雲神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11388585}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11388585}} [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]]

[[commons:Category:Terumo-jinja]][[en:Terumo Shrine]][[ja:光雲神社]] [[sn:commons:Category:Terumo-jinja]][[en:Terumo Shrine]][[ja:光雲神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11388585]] Wikidata name: Terumo Jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Fukuoka City (translation of 光雲神社)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Terumo Shrine
光雲神社 | image = File:Fukuoka Terumo shrine 2010A.jpg | location = 13-1 Nishi Park, Chūō-ku, Fukuoka City, Fukuoka Prefecture {{location map|Japan Fukuoka Prefecture | alt = Terumo Shrine (Fukuoka Prefecture) | coordinates = {{coord|33|35|50|N|130|22|35|E}} }} | coordinates = {{Coord|33.59748611111111|N|130.3765388888889|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = {{ill|Chūō-ku|WD=Q1156273}}| founded_by = {{ill|Kuroda Tsugutaka|WD=Q11678674}}| deity = {{ill|Kuroda Nagamasa|WD=Q950496}}; {{ill|Kuroda Yoshitaka|WD=Q907019}}}} {{Nihongo|'''Terumo Shrine'''|光雲神社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Fukuoka City|Fukuoka City]]. Latterly located in Nishi Park in the [[:en:Chūō-ku, Fukuoka|Chūō-ku ward]] of the city, the shrine and all contents were destroyed in 1945; a reconstruction in modern materials was erected in 1966. ==Summary== Terumo Shrine was classified as a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines#Prefectural shrines|prefectural shrine]] but is now classified as a Beppyo shrine by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It enshrines [[:en:Kuroda Yoshitaka|Kuroda Yoshitaka]], the founder of the historical [[:en:Fukuoka Domain|Fukuoka Domain]], and his son, [[:en:Kuroda Nagamasa|Kuroda Nagamasa]], the first [[daimyo|daimyō]] of the domain. The name was derived from the second characters of the posthumous Buddhist names of those enshrined; 光 (light) from Yoshitaka, and 雲 (cloud) from Nagamasa. On the grounds, there is a statue of Mori Tomonobu, a feudal retainer of the Fukuoka Domain, and one of the twenty-four Kuroda Horsemen. Below the statue, a folk song based on Mori called ‘[[:en:Kuroda Bushi|Kuroda Bushi]]’ is engraved. There is also a sculpture of Nagamasa’s favored horned helmet...{{Cite web |url=https://www.terumojinja.com/ |title=光雲神社公式ホームページ |accessdate=2022-04-06 |publisher=光雲神社 |archive-date=2022-04-04 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220404154132/https://www.terumojinja.com/ |url-status=live }} The ceiling of the front shine is decorated with a painting by Shin Kihara called 謡鶴 (''utaidzuru'', ‘Singing Cranes’), which makes the call of a crane when an offering is given{{Cite web|url=https://dayori.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/161105/|title=中央区ものしり手帳　第３話 光雲神社のさい銭箱|accessdate=2022-06-01|publisher=[[福岡市]]|archive-date=2020-12-05|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201205173357/https://dayori.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/161105/|url-status=live}} In Kakiha Shrine, an auxiliary shrine of Terumo Shrine, Kuroda Shigetaka, [[:en:Kuroda Mototaka|Kuroda Mototaka]], Kuroda Tadayuki, Kuroda Tsunayuki, Atsuhime (daughter of Kuroda Tsugutaka and fiancée of Daigo Fuyuyoshi), and other related figures are enshrined as [[kami]]. The plaque on the front of the shrine bearing its name was designed by Kuroda Nagahisa, the fifteenth head of the [[:en:Kuroda clan|Kuroda clan]]. The road to this shrine, affectionately known as ‘Nishi Park’s Sakura Path’ meets with Japan National Route 556, which passes between 3-chōme and 2-chōme of the Arato district of Fukuoka City{{Cite web |url=https://www.nishinippon.co.jp/item/n/777786/ |title=西公園さくら参道の紹介記事 |accessdate=2022-04-06 |publisher=[[西日本新聞]] |archive-date=2021-12-05 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211205132813/https://www.nishinippon.co.jp/item/n/777786/ |url-status=live }} ==History== {{multiple image | align = right | width = 220 | image1 = Cherry blossoms and torii of Aratsu Shrine in Terumo Shrine.JPG | caption1 = Aratsu Shrine in Terumo Shrine | image2 = Cherry blossoms and torii of Kakiwa Shrine in Terumo Shrine.JPG | caption2 = Kakiwa Shrine in Terumo Shrine }} In 1766, the sixth daimyō of the Fukuoka domain, Kuroda Tsugutaka, built a [[:en:Hokora|Hokora]] in the outer citadel of Fukuoka Castle to worship Nagamasa. Soon afterwards, Yoshitaka was enshrined there as well. In 1871, the Kuroda family moved to [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] after the [[:en:Abolition of the han system|abolition of the feudal domain system]] and the implementation of the [[:en:Prefectures of Japan|prefecture system]]. Interested parties petitioned the eleventh daimyō, Kuroda Nagahiro, to move the shrine from inside the castle to downtown Fukuoka. In 1875, it was classified as a prefectural shrine. The shrine was moved again to its current location in Nishi Park in 1909. This location once had Aratoyama Tōshōgū, a shrine built by Kuroda Tadayuki. According to records of the Fukuoka Domain, the Aratoyama Tōshōgū had the most magnificent shrine buildings, surpassing the various [[:en:Tōshōgū|Tōshō-gūs]] (shrines venerating [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]]) across Japan. In 1945, the [[:en:Bombing of Fukuoka|Bombing of Fukuoka]] completely destroyed Terumo Shrine. Documents, swords, and other treasures connected to the Kuroda family, and the main shrine building were lost in the resulting fires. In 1966, the shrine was rebuilt with concrete. ==References== {{Reflist}} == External links == ・[https://www.terumojinja.com/ 光雲神社]（公式サイト） {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] }} [[en:Terumo Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Terumo-jinja}} [[ja:光雲神社]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11388585}}

[[commons:Category:Terumo-jinja]][[en:Terumo Shrine]][[ja:光雲神社]] [[commons:Category:Terumo-jinja]][[en:Terumo Shrine]][[ja:光雲神社]] [[sn:commons:Category:Terumo-jinja]][[en:Terumo Shrine]][[ja:光雲神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11388585]] Wikidata name: Terumo Jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Fukuoka City (translation of 光雲神社)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Terumo Shrine

Below the statue, a folk song based on Mori called ‘[[:en:Kuroda Bushi|Kuroda Bushi]]’ is engraved. There is also a sculpture of Nagamasa’s favored horned helmet...{{Cite web |url=https://www.terumojinja.com/ |title=光雲神社公式ホームページ |accessdate=2022-04-06 |publisher=光雲神社 |archive-date=2022-04-04 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220404154132/https://www.terumojinja.com/ |url-status=live }} The ceiling of the front shine is decorated with a painting by Shin Kihara called 謡鶴 (''utaidzuru'', ‘Singing Cranes’), which makes the call of a crane when an offering is given{{Cite web|url=https://dayori.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/161105/|title=中央区ものしり手帳　第３話 光雲神社のさい銭箱|accessdate=2022-06-01|publisher=[[福岡市]]|archive-date=2020-12-05|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201205173357/https://dayori.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/161105/|url-status=live}} In Kakiha Shrine, an auxiliary shrine of Terumo Shrine, Kuroda Shigetaka, [[:en:Kuroda Mototaka|Kuroda Mototaka]], Kuroda Tadayuki, Kuroda Tsunayuki, Atsuhime (daughter of Kuroda Tsugutaka and fiancée of Daigo Fuyuyoshi), and other related figures are enshrined as [[kami]]. The road to this shrine, affectionately known as ‘Nishi Park’s Sakura Path’ meets with Japan National Route 556, which passes between 3-chōme and 2-chōme of the Arato district of Fukuoka City{{Cite web |url=https://www.nishinippon.co.jp/item/n/777786/ |title=西公園さくら参道の紹介記事 |accessdate=2022-04-06 |publisher=[[西日本新聞]] |archive-date=2021-12-05 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211205132813/https://www.nishinippon.co.jp/item/n/777786/ |url-status=live }} ==History== {{multiple image | align = right | width = 220 | image1 = Cherry blossoms and torii of Aratsu Shrine in Terumo Shrine.JPG | caption1 = Aratsu Shrine in Terumo Shrine | image2 = Cherry blossoms and torii of Kakiwa Shrine in Terumo Shrine.JPG | caption2 = Kakiwa Shrine in Terumo Shrine }} In 1766, the sixth daimyō of the Fukuoka domain, Kuroda Tsugutaka, built a [[:en:Hokora|Hokora]] in the outer citadel of Fukuoka Castle to worship Nagamasa. Soon afterwards, Yoshitaka was enshrined there as well. In 1871, the Kuroda family moved to [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] after the [[:en:Abolition of the han system|abolition of the feudal domain system]] and the implementation of the [[:en:Prefectures of Japan|prefecture system]]. Interested parties petitioned the eleventh daimyō, Kuroda Nagahiro, to move the shrine from inside the castle to downtown Fukuoka. In 1875, it was classified as a prefectural shrine. The shrine was moved again to its current location in Nishi Park in 1909. This location once had Aratoyama Tōshōgū, a shrine built by Kuroda Tadayuki. According to records of the Fukuoka Domain, the Aratoyama Tōshōgū had the most magnificent shrine buildings, surpassing the various [[:en:Tōshōgū|Tōshō-gūs]] (shrines venerating [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]]) across Japan. In 1945, the [[:en:Bombing of Fukuoka|Bombing of Fukuoka]] completely destroyed Terumo Shrine. Documents, swords, and other treasures connected to the Kuroda family, and the main shrine building were lost in the resulting fires. In 1966, the shrine was rebuilt with concrete. ==References== {{Reflist}} == External links == ・[https://www.terumojinja.com/ 光雲神社]（公式サイト） {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| }} [[en:Terumo Shrine]] {{Commons category|Terumo-jinja}} [[ja:光雲神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11388585}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

光雲神社 | image = File:Fukuoka Terumo shrine 2010A.jpg | location = 13-1 Nishi Park, Chūō-ku, Fukuoka City, Fukuoka Prefecture {{location map|Japan Fukuoka Prefecture | alt = Terumo Shrine (Fukuoka Prefecture) | coordinates = {{coord|33|35|50|N|130|22|35|E}} }} | coordinates = {{Coord|33.59748611111111|N|130.3765388888889|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = {{ill|Chūō-ku|WD=Q1156273}}| founded_by = {{ill|Kuroda Tsugutaka|WD=Q11678674}}| deity = {{ill|Kuroda Nagamasa|WD=Q950496}}; {{ill|Kuroda Yoshitaka|WD=Q907019}}}} {{Nihongo|'''Terumo Shrine'''|光雲神社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Fukuoka City|Fukuoka City]]. Latterly located in Nishi Park in the [[:en:Chūō-ku, Fukuoka|Chūō-ku ward]] of the city, the shrine and all contents were destroyed in 1945; a reconstruction in modern materials was erected in 1966. ==Summary== Terumo Shrine was classified as a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines#Prefectural shrines|prefectural shrine]] but is now classified as a Beppyo shrine by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It enshrines [[:en:Kuroda Yoshitaka|Kuroda Yoshitaka]], the founder of the historical [[:en:Fukuoka Domain|Fukuoka Domain]], and his son, [[:en:Kuroda Nagamasa|Kuroda Nagamasa]], the first [[daimyo|daimyō]] of the domain. The name was derived from the second characters of the posthumous Buddhist names of those enshrined; 光 (light) from Yoshitaka, and 雲 (cloud) from Nagamasa. The plaque on the front of the shrine bearing its name was designed by Kuroda Nagahisa, the fifteenth head of the [[:en:Kuroda clan|Kuroda clan]]. The shrine was moved again to its current location in Nishi Park in 1909. This location once had Aratoyama Tōshōgū, a shrine built by Kuroda Tadayuki. According to records of the Fukuoka Domain, the Aratoyama Tōshōgū had the most magnificent shrine buildings, surpassing the various [[:en:Tōshōgū|Tōshō-gūs]] (shrines venerating [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]]) across Japan. 光雲神社 | image = File:Fukuoka Terumo shrine 2010A.jpg | location = 13-1 Nishi Park, Chūō-ku, Fukuoka City, Fukuoka Prefecture {{location map|Japan Fukuoka Prefecture | alt = Terumo Shrine (Fukuoka Prefecture) | coordinates = {{coord|33|35|50|N|130|22|35|E}} }} | coordinates = {{Coord|33.59748611111111|N|130.3765388888889|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = {{ill|Chūō-ku|WD=Q1156273}}| founded_by = {{ill|Kuroda Tsugutaka|WD=Q11678674}}| deity = {{ill|Kuroda Nagamasa|WD=Q950496}}; {{ill|Kuroda Yoshitaka|WD=Q907019}}}} {{Nihongo|'''Terumo Shrine'''|光雲神社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Fukuoka City|Fukuoka City]]. Latterly located in Nishi Park in the [[:en:Chūō-ku, Fukuoka|Chūō-ku ward]] of the city, the shrine and all contents were destroyed in 1945; a reconstruction in modern materials was erected in 1966. ==Summary== Terumo Shrine was classified as a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines#Prefectural shrines|prefectural shrine]] but is now classified as a Beppyo shrine by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It enshrines [[:en:Kuroda Yoshitaka|Kuroda Yoshitaka]], the founder of the historical [[:en:Fukuoka Domain|Fukuoka Domain]], and his son, [[:en:Kuroda Nagamasa|Kuroda Nagamasa]], the first [[daimyo|daimyō]] of the domain. The name was derived from the second characters of the posthumous Buddhist names of those enshrined; 光 (light) from Yoshitaka, and 雲 (cloud) from Nagamasa. On the grounds, there is a statue of Mori Tomonobu, a feudal retainer of the Fukuoka Domain, and one of the twenty-four Kuroda Horsemen. Below the statue, a folk song based on Mori called ‘[[:en:Kuroda Bushi|Kuroda Bushi]]’ is engraved. There is also a sculpture of Nagamasa’s favored horned helmet...{{Cite web |url=https://www.terumojinja.com/ |title=光雲神社公式ホームページ |accessdate=2022-04-06 |publisher=光雲神社 |archive-date=2022-04-04 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220404154132/https://www.terumojinja.com/ |url-status=live }} The ceiling of the front shine is decorated with a painting by Shin Kihara called 謡鶴 (''utaidzuru'', ‘Singing Cranes’), which makes the call of a crane when an offering is given{{Cite web|url=https://dayori.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/161105/|title=中央区ものしり手帳　第３話 光雲神社のさい銭箱|accessdate=2022-06-01|publisher=[[福岡市]]|archive-date=2020-12-05|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201205173357/https://dayori.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/161105/|url-status=live}} In Kakiha Shrine, an auxiliary shrine of Terumo Shrine, Kuroda Shigetaka, [[:en:Kuroda Mototaka|Kuroda Mototaka]], Kuroda Tadayuki, Kuroda Tsunayuki, Atsuhime (daughter of Kuroda Tsugutaka and fiancée of Daigo Fuyuyoshi), and other related figures are enshrined as [[kami]]. The plaque on the front of the shrine bearing its name was designed by Kuroda Nagahisa, the fifteenth head of the [[:en:Kuroda clan|Kuroda clan]]. The road to this shrine, affectionately known as ‘Nishi Park’s Sakura Path’ meets with Japan National Route 556, which passes between 3-chōme and 2-chōme of the Arato district of Fukuoka City{{Cite web |url=https://www.nishinippon.co.jp/item/n/777786/ |title=西公園さくら参道の紹介記事 |accessdate=2022-04-06 |publisher=[[西日本新聞]] |archive-date=2021-12-05 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211205132813/https://www.nishinippon.co.jp/item/n/777786/ |url-status=live }} ==History== {{multiple image | align = right | width = 220 | image1 = Cherry blossoms and torii of Aratsu Shrine in Terumo Shrine.JPG | caption1 = Aratsu Shrine in Terumo Shrine | image2 = Cherry blossoms and torii of Kakiwa Shrine in Terumo Shrine.JPG | caption2 = Kakiwa Shrine in Terumo Shrine }} In 1766, the sixth daimyō of the Fukuoka domain, Kuroda Tsugutaka, built a [[:en:Hokora|Hokora]] in the outer citadel of Fukuoka Castle to worship Nagamasa. Soon afterwards, Yoshitaka was enshrined there as well. In 1871, the Kuroda family moved to [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] after the [[:en:Abolition of the han system|abolition of the feudal domain system]] and the implementation of the [[:en:Prefectures of Japan|prefecture system]]. Interested parties petitioned the eleventh daimyō, Kuroda Nagahiro, to move the shrine from inside the castle to downtown Fukuoka. In 1875, it was classified as a prefectural shrine. The shrine was moved again to its current location in Nishi Park in 1909. This location once had Aratoyama Tōshōgū, a shrine built by Kuroda Tadayuki. According to records of the Fukuoka Domain, the Aratoyama Tōshōgū had the most magnificent shrine buildings, surpassing the various [[:en:Tōshōgū|Tōshō-gūs]] (shrines venerating [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]]) across Japan. In 1945, the [[:en:Bombing of Fukuoka|Bombing of Fukuoka]] completely destroyed Terumo Shrine. Documents, swords, and other treasures connected to the Kuroda family, and the main shrine building were lost in the resulting fires. In 1966, the shrine was rebuilt with concrete. ==References== {{Reflist}} == External links == ・[https://www.terumojinja.com/ 光雲神社]（公式サイト） {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[en:Terumo Shrine]] {{Commons category|Terumo-jinja}} [[ja:光雲神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11388585}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
